course.title: Intelligent Agents
course.subtitle: PPGI · IC / UFAL · 2026.1
course.tagline: From the past to the future of AI agents

sections.name: Past
sections.description: The roots of intelligent agents, a timeline from 1950 to 2000 in three eras: the birth of symbolic AI (1950–1970), cognitive architectures and the symbolic-vs-reactive clash (1980–1990), and multi-agent systems, communication languages, and standards (1990–2000).

children.name: Timeline
children.description: The section's map: half a century of intelligent agents (1950–2000) told in three eras, and the through-line that connects them.
The birth of AI: from the mathematical neuron and the Turing Test to symbolic AI, microworlds and planning robots, expert systems, and the convergence of control theory and classical AI.

children.name: Foundations
children.description: Overview and timeline of 1950–1970, and the period's central question: how to mechanize goal-directed behavior.

children.name: The Birth of AI
children.description: The McCulloch & Pitts neuron, the Turing Test, and the Dartmouth Conference that named the field and gave rise to symbolic AI.

children.name: Symbolic Era & GPS
children.description: The Symbolic Era: the Logic Theorist and the General Problem Solver, the dream of a universal problem solver, and the limit of combinatorial explosion.

children.name: Microworlds & Planning Robots
children.description: Microworlds and the first planning robots: SHRDLU in the Blocks World, the robot Shakey, and the STRIPS planning algorithm.

children.name: Expert Systems
children.description: The epistemological turn and expert systems: production architecture, DENDRAL, MYCIN and XCON, and the limitations that led to machine learning.

children.name: Control Theory & Classical AI
children.description: Cybernetics, feedback, and control theory versus classical AI, and the perception → planning → action convergence that prefigured the modern agent.

children.name: References
children.description: The bibliography cited in the 1950–1970 presentation, from the founding papers of 1943–1959 to the classic surveys.

The architecture of the artificial mind: the agent as an abstraction, the ACT-R and SOAR cognitive architectures, the BDI/PRS model (all the way to NASA's Space Shuttle), and Brooks's reactive critique that led into the AI Winter.

children.name: Foundations
children.description: Overview of 1980–1990: the institutionalization of AI, the agent abstraction, and the tension between top-down (symbolic) and bottom-up (reactive) approaches.

children.name: Architecture of the Artificial Mind
children.description: Defining intelligence as an agent, the institutionalization of AI, the era's two currents, and the ACT-R cognitive architecture validated with fMRI.

children.name: SOAR
children.description: The SOAR cognitive architecture (Newell, Laird & Rosenbloom, 1983): reasoning as search in a problem space, and chunking as automatic learning.

children.name: BDI
children.description: Bratman's Belief–Desire–Intention model (1987), the PRS framework, and its most famous application: the fault-detection system of NASA's Space Shuttle.

children.name: Production Systems & Reactive Critique
children.description: The commercial peak of symbolic AI (OPS-5, R1/XCON) and Rodney Brooks's rupture: intelligence without representation, the subsumption architecture, and the AI Winter.

children.name: References
children.description: The bibliography cited in the 1980–1990 presentation: SOAR, ACT-R, BDI/PRS, and general references.

The social decade of agents: the Contract Net Protocol for negotiation, Agent-Oriented Programming, the speech-act-based communication languages (KQML, FIPA-ACL), and the JADE platform that made it all executable.

children.name: Foundations
children.description: Overview of 1990–2000: the shift from cognition to communication, multi-agent systems, and the thread linking negotiation, communication languages, standards, and platforms.

children.name: Contract Net Protocol
children.description: Reid Smith's protocol for dynamically distributing tasks among agents: the announce → bid → award → execute cycle, inspired by the business contract market.

children.name: Agent-Oriented Programming
children.description: Yoav Shoham's AOP (1989): a restriction of OOP in which the agent has a mental state (beliefs, capabilities, commitments) and communicates through speech acts, implemented in the AGENT-0 language.

children.name: Agent Communication & KQML
children.description: The problem of the 1990s, Genesereth's vision, the philosophical base of speech acts (Austin/Searle), and KQML, the first widely used agent communication language.

children.name: FIPA-ACL
children.description: The FIPA-ACL standard: formal modal-logic semantics (feasibility preconditions + rational effect), the communicative acts, and the comparison with KQML.

children.name: JADE
children.description: The Java Agent Development Framework: open-source, FIPA-compliant middleware with a distributed architecture, white/yellow pages, life cycle, behaviours, and the Contract Net ready to use.

children.name: References
children.description: The bibliography cited in the 1990–2000 presentation: Contract Net, speech acts, KQML, FIPA-ACL, JADE, and references from the earlier eras.

sections.name: Present
sections.description: The state of the art of AI agents: usage guidelines, engineering best practices, hands-on experiments, and the current tooling around Claude Code and equivalent platforms.

children.name: Guidelines
children.description: Guidelines for understanding, designing, and operating intelligent agents responsibly, from architecture to the context window.

children.name: Synthesis
children.description: A synthesis of the guidelines for intelligent agents: architecture, memory, tools, and stopping criteria.

children.name: Multi-Platform
children.description: How skills, rules, prompts, and agents map across Claude, Copilot, Gemini, and Cursor, with a decision diagram.

children.name: Best Practices
children.description: Consolidated patterns for working with agents: spec-driven development, subagents, and team formation.

children.name: Spec-Driven Development
children.description: Spec-driven development with agents: from the contract (spec) to the plan, the tasks, and a verifiable implementation.

children.name: Subagents
children.description: When and how to delegate to subagents: context isolation, parallelism, and adversarial verification.

children.name: Courses
children.description: Courses and reference materials on workflows with coding agents.

children.name: Experiments
children.description: Hands-on experiments conducted in the course, exploring the limits and capabilities of agents.

children.name: Subagents
children.description: Experiments with subagents: orchestration, fan-out, and measuring results.

children.name: Skills
children.description: Experiments with skills: conventional commits, a protective hook, and automation with Playwright.

children.name: Conventional Commit Skill
children.description: The conventional-commit skill used in Experiment 1.

children.name: Git Commit Skill
children.description: The ambiguous commit skill used in Experiment 1.

children.name: Hook
children.description: The protective hook triggered in Experiment 1.

children.name: Articles · Skills
children.description: A collection of articles about skills: what they are, how they work, and why they matter.

children.name: Claude Code & Tools
children.description: Claude Code, Claude Cowork, and Claude Design commands, the tooling for day-to-day work.

children.name: Costs
children.description: Costs, tokens, and efficiency comparisons across models and platforms.

sections.name: Future
sections.description: Where agents are heading: from language-based software assistants to multimodal systems that perceive, model, and plan in the physical world. World models in the JEPA lineage (V-JEPA 2 → LeWorldModel → HWM), latent-space planning, and the transition from Generative AI to Physical AI.

children.name: Foundations
children.description: The conceptual bridge from present to future: from language-based software agents to multimodal agents that perceive, model, predict, and plan in the world.

children.name: Articles · World Models
children.description: A per-article summary of the LeCun/JEPA lineage, from the 2022 manifesto paper to the 2026 world models and distributed agentic AI at the edge.

children.name: Best Practices
children.description: The overview from both presentations: the three eras of AI, autonomy levels, world-model architecture, hierarchical planning, risks, and governance.

children.name: Experiments
children.description: A digest of the benchmarks reported in the papers (HWM, Temporal Straightening, LeWorldModel, V-JEPA 2.1) with metric tables and the cross-cutting analysis.

children.name: Hands-On
children.description: Practical access to world models: which models to run, how to test them, video demos, industrial deployments, and step-by-step guides per model.

children.name: V-JEPA 2
children.description: Video encoder + zero-shot robot planning. Colab, HuggingFace, PyTorch Hub.

children.name: I-JEPA
children.description: The simplest entry point for understanding JEPA: 5 lines on HuggingFace, runs on CPU.

children.name: LeWorldModel
children.description: A complete train-predict-plan pipeline on a single GPU in a few hours. The most accessible JEPA.

children.name: DreamerV3
children.description: Training agents in imagination: Nature 2025, Minecraft diamond, a single hyperparameter set.

children.name: DreamerV4
children.description: Playing inside a world model in real time with a joystick. Interactive demos.

children.name: NVIDIA Cosmos 3
children.description: An omnimodal world model (text + image + video + action). NIM containers, production-grade.

children.name: NVIDIA Isaac Sim
children.description: Robotics simulation, digital twins, sim-to-real. Free, an industry standard.

children.name: World Labs Marble
children.description: 3D world generation in the browser: no GPU, no code. API available.

children.name: Costs
children.description: Cost as compute and energy: 48× faster latent planning, model sizes, training/data cost, and the energy frontier at the edge.
